explanation: The statement says: In Standby Mode, it should be possible (i.e., there exists a path or a way) to achieve prop_1 before both prop_2 and prop_3. In standard LTL, satisfaction means the property holds on all paths; 'possible' is typically interpreted as 'there exists a path', which LTL alone cannot express, but we can express that there is no temporal obligation for prop_2 or prop_3 to always come before prop_1. However, what we *can* directly express is that it is **never** required for prop_2 or prop_3 to always come before prop_1. Alternatively, we can use the 'possible' notion as: 'It is not always the case that either prop_2 or prop_3 occur before prop_1.' In LTL: 'Not globally: (if prop_2 or prop_3, then sometime earlier prop_1)'. That is, it is possible that prop_1 happens before either prop_2 or prop_3.
output_LTL: !G((F((breathingCircuitConnected | patientConnected)) | F((ventilating))) -> F((ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds) U ((breathingCircuitConnected | patientConnected) | (ventilating))))

explanation: The requirement states that it shall be possible to achieve prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3, under the condition of prop_0. 'It shall be possible to' translates to 'eventually', i.e., 'F', in LTL. Thus, under prop_0, eventually prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 can hold. Typically, this reads as: if prop_0 holds, then eventually prop_1, prop_2, and prop_3 must all be possible (achievable), which can be captured as: G(prop_0 -> F(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3)).

The requirement states that in Standby Mode, it must be possible for prop_1 to occur before both prop_2 and prop_3. In LTL, 'possible' is interpreted as 'there exists a path where...'. Since basic LTL is universal (over all paths), to express 'possibility', we normally encode that there is no global constraint forbidding this order. For this translation, we can require that it is NOT always the case that either prop_2 or prop_3 occurs before prop_1, i.e., it is not globally true that both prop_2 and prop_3 must come before prop_1. This can be captured as: 'It is not globally true that (prop_2 or prop_3) happens before prop_1' (which is ¬G( (¬prop_1) U (prop_2 | prop_3) )), which is equivalent to: Eventually, it is possible that prop_1 happens before either prop_2 or prop_3.

The requirement is that in Standby Mode, prop_0 must happen before both prop_1 and prop_2. In LTL, 'before' can be encoded by saying that neither prop_1 nor prop_2 can occur until after prop_0 occurs. This can be expressed as: (!prop_1 & !prop_2) U prop_0. If you need to scope this only to Standby Mode, you would need an atomic proposition indicating Standby Mode, but since it is not listed, I am assuming 'Standby Mode' is just the context, and the logic focuses on prop_0, prop_1, and prop_2.

The statement indicates that, in Standby Mode, prop_0 can occur before either prop_1 or prop_2 occur, but it does not require that prop_0 must occur before both—only that it is possible. This means that there exists a path where, from the start, neither prop_1 nor prop_2 have happened yet, and then prop_0 can occur before either of them. In LTL, possibility is typically expressed by 'eventually (prop_0 happens and up to this point neither prop_1 nor prop_2 have happened)'.

The phrase 'it shall be possible to prop_0 before prop_1 and prop_2' means there exists some execution (i.e., possibility, which is expressed as 'eventually' in LTL) in which prop_0 happens prior to either prop_1 or prop_2. Put differently, there must exist a path in which neither prop_1 nor prop_2 has occurred before prop_0. The LTL formula expresses that it is possible (i.e., 'eventually') to have prop_0 occur without any previous occurrence of prop_1 or prop_2.
output_LTL: F ((ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds) & G (!((patientConnected & breathingCircuitConnected) | (ventilating)) U (ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds)))

explanation:
The requirement says that in Standby Mode, it must be possible for prop_1 to occur before both prop_2 and prop_3 (i.e., there is an execution where prop_1 happens before either prop_2 or prop_3 occur). Since LTL expresses necessity by default, to express 'possible' in LTL, we use negation: 'it is possible that p' means 'it is not necessarily the case that not p'. So, we can say: it is not necessarily the case that, if Standby Mode is active, then prop_2 or prop_3 occurs before prop_1. 

Let S be 'Standby Mode'. Since 'S' is not one of the atomic propositions, I'll assume 'in Standby Mode' is implicit and express only the possibility aspect: 'it is possible for prop_1 to occur before both prop_2 and prop_3.'

This can be formalized as: it is not globally true that if prop_2 or prop_3 occurs, then prop_1 must have already occurred. Thus, there exists at least one path where prop_1 occurs before prop_2 and prop_3.

¬G((prop_2 | prop_3) -> F[0:1] prop_1)

But to directly encode that 'it is possible for prop_1 to happen before prop_2 and prop_3', we can express: there is an execution in which prop_1 holds and before any prop_2 or prop_3.

That is, F(prop_1 & G((!prop_2 & !prop_3) U prop_1))
output_LTL: F((ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged) & G((!(breathingCircuitConnected & patientConnected) & !(ventilating & !StandbyMode)) U (ventilationParamsAdjustable & alarmSettingsChanged)))

explanation: The requirement states that, whenever it is Standby Mode, it must be possible (i.e., possible along some path) for prop_1 to occur before both prop_2 and prop_3. In standard LTL, we describe sufficiency properties (i.e., what must always or eventually happen) rather than possibility in the sense of existential paths, but often what is meant in these requirements is that there is no required ordering forcing prop_2 or prop_3 before prop_1. We can express that in every Standby Mode, it is *not* the case that prop_2 or prop_3 must happen before prop_1. That is, whenever we are in Standby Mode and prop_2 or prop_3 has not occurred, prop_1 can still occur before them. The LTL given ensures that in Standby Mode, if neither prop_2 nor prop_3 is true yet, then until (U) prop_1 occurs, prop_2 nor prop_3 must not occur first: that is, prop_1 can occur before either of them.

The property specifies that when in Standby Mode, prop_0 must occur before both prop_1 and prop_2. This means that neither prop_1 nor prop_2 should happen until after prop_0 has occurred. In LTL, this can be expressed as: until either prop_1 or prop_2 occurs, prop_0 has not yet occurred, or after prop_0 occurs first, then prop_1 or prop_2 may occur. This can be captured using the 'until' operator, ensuring that prop_1 and prop_2 do not occur until after prop_0 occurs.

This requirement says that, when in Standby Mode (which we assume is encoded in the context, not as an atomic proposition here), it must be possible (i.e., not necessarily the case, but allowed) that prop_0 occurs before both prop_1 and prop_2. In LTL, to say that it is 'possible' means that there exists a path where this happens. LTL, being linear, does not support explicit existential quantification over paths, but we can express 'it is not globally the case that prop_1 or prop_2 always appear before prop_0'. That is, it is not required that 'whenever prop_1 or prop_2 occurs, prop_0 has already occurred', so there must be a path where prop_0 occurs before either of them. To express that it is possible for prop_0 to occur before prop_1 and prop_2, we can use the formula 'F ( prop_0 & !prop_1 & !prop_2 & ( (!prop_1 U prop_0) & (!prop_2 U prop_0) ) )', which means: Eventually, there will be a point where prop_0 occurs, and before that, neither prop_1 nor prop_2 have occurred.
output_LTL: F ( (!(patientConnected & breathingCircuitConnected) U (ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds)) & (!(patientConnected & ventilating) U (ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds)) & (ventilationParamsAdjustable & enterAlarmThresholds) )
